---
title: "Matching and Weighting Sources"
---

# Scope

This note is a source map for matching and weighting reading. It is organized around the kinds of sources most worth taking notes on: overviews, benchmark papers, method papers, and software manuals [@cunningham2018matching; @gertler2010ch8matching; @ch3evaluation; @lalonde1986evaluating; @dehejia1999causal; @abadie2011biascorrected; @iacus2012cem; @hainmueller2012entropy; @ho2011matchit; @greifer2025weightit; @imbens2025comparingexperimentalnonexperimentalmethods].

# Start Here

If you want a compact starting stack, I would begin with these four:

1. Cunningham, *Causal Inference: The Mixtape*, for the best intuition-first overview [@cunningham2018matching].
2. Gertler et al., *Impact Evaluation in Practice*, for the clearest evaluator-facing workflow [@gertler2010ch8matching].
3. LaLonde (1986) and Dehejia and Wahba (1999), because the benchmark debate still structures how matching designs are judged [@lalonde1986evaluating; @dehejia1999causal].
4. One implementation pair: the `MatchIt` paper for matching workflows and the `WeightIt` package manual for weighting workflows [@ho2011matchit; @greifer2025weightit].

That combination gives you design logic, benchmark discipline, and practical software entry points.

# Textbooks And Broad Overviews

## Cunningham (2018), *Causal Inference: The Mixtape*, chapter on matching and subclassification

This is the best single overview if the goal is to understand what matching is trying to do conceptually and why it is a design-stage method rather than a button-click exercise [@cunningham2018matching].

Why it is worth notes:

- it explains matching in causal-inference language rather than only as an optimization problem
- it is shorter and easier to absorb quickly than the formal estimator papers
- it connects naturally to the benchmark examples used elsewhere in the repo

## Gertler et al. (2010), *Impact Evaluation in Practice*, chapter on matching

This is the strongest practical overview if the audience is evaluator-facing rather than econometrics-facing [@gertler2010ch8matching].

Why it is worth notes:

- it explains matching as constructing an artificial comparison group
- it emphasizes overlap, covariate choice, and unobserved-confounding risk
- it is a good bridge from basic matching to matched DiD and synthetic control

## Magenta Book, chapter on evaluation methods

This is not a deep matching source, but it is useful if the notes need a UK-government framing of where matching and weighting sit among quasi-experimental methods [@ch3evaluation].

Why it is worth notes:

- it places matching and weighting inside a broader method-choice workflow
- it is useful for evaluator-facing explanation rather than technical depth
- it connects method selection to practical policy evaluation

# Benchmark Papers And Reassessments

## LaLonde (1986)

This is the canonical benchmark source for thinking seriously about observational adjustment [@lalonde1986evaluating].

Why it is worth notes:

- it made the benchmark problem concrete rather than abstract
- it shows why naive nonexperimental adjustment can fail badly
- it still anchors later debates about whether matching actually recovers credible treatment effects

## Dehejia and Wahba (1999)

This is the classic paper for the “design can improve observational estimates” side of the LaLonde debate [@dehejia1999causal].

Why it is worth notes:

- it made propensity-score-based design central to the benchmark literature
- it shows how covariate choice and overlap discipline affect results
- it is a useful entry point into the post-LaLonde reanalysis tradition

## Imbens and Xu (2025)

This is the best recent reassessment if the goal is to understand what the LaLonde benchmark still teaches now [@imbens2025comparingexperimentalnonexperimentalmethods].

Why it is worth notes:

- it summarizes four decades of matching and weighting debates
- it keeps the focus on overlap, validation, and design assumptions rather than estimator branding
- it is especially useful for notes on what modern practice should still learn from benchmark exercises

# Matching-Specific Method Papers

## Abadie and Imbens (2011)

This is the key paper if your notes need to move from intuition to the statistical behavior of matching estimators [@abadie2011biascorrected].

Why it is worth notes:

- it clarifies why bias correction matters for matching estimators
- it helps distinguish simple nearest-neighbor practice from more careful estimation
- it is important once the notes need to say more than “find similar units”

## Iacus, King, and Porro (2012)

This is the main source to read if you want a serious note on coarsened exact matching [@iacus2012cem].

Why it is worth notes:

- it gives `CEM` its clearest methodological statement
- it is useful for explaining why coarsening can make matching more transparent
- it helps separate exact-style design logic from propensity-score workflows

# Weighting-Specific Method Papers

## Hainmueller (2012)

This is the key entropy-balancing paper [@hainmueller2012entropy].

Why it is worth notes:

- it states the method clearly as a balance-constrained reweighting problem
- it is the main source if you want to explain weighting without reducing everything to propensity scores
- it is especially relevant for ATT-style “build a weighted control group” workflows

# Software Manuals And Implementation Sources

## `MatchIt`

The foundational implementation source for matching workflows in R is the `MatchIt` paper [@ho2011matchit].

Take notes on:

- matching as preprocessing before outcome analysis
- exact matching, nearest-neighbor matching, and subclassification workflows
- the role of balance diagnostics rather than estimator-brand loyalty

## `WeightIt`

Use `WeightIt` when your notes need to cover weighting workflows in a unified interface [@greifer2025weightit].

Take notes on:

- estimands such as `ATT`, `ATE`, and `ATC`
- inverse probability weighting, overlap weighting, and entropy balancing
- effective sample size and weight concentration diagnostics

# Suggested Note-Taking Order

If you want a sequence that compounds well, use:

1. Cunningham on matching and subclassification [@cunningham2018matching].
2. Gertler et al. on matching [@gertler2010ch8matching].
3. LaLonde (1986) and Dehejia-Wahba (1999) [@lalonde1986evaluating; @dehejia1999causal].
4. Imbens and Xu (2025) for the modern benchmark reassessment [@imbens2025comparingexperimentalnonexperimentalmethods].
5. `MatchIt` and Abadie-Imbens for matching implementation and estimator behavior [@ho2011matchit; @abadie2011biascorrected].
6. Hainmueller (2012) and `WeightIt` for weighting workflows [@hainmueller2012entropy; @greifer2025weightit].
7. Iacus, King, and Porro (2012) if you want a separate note on `CEM` [@iacus2012cem].

# Bottom Line

If I were deciding what to take notes on first for matching and weighting, I would prioritize four source types:

- one intuition-first overview: Cunningham [@cunningham2018matching]
- one evaluator-facing overview: Gertler et al. [@gertler2010ch8matching]
- one benchmark thread: LaLonde, Dehejia-Wahba, and Imbens-Xu [@lalonde1986evaluating; @dehejia1999causal; @imbens2025comparingexperimentalnonexperimentalmethods]
- one implementation pair: `MatchIt` for matching and `WeightIt` for weighting [@ho2011matchit; @greifer2025weightit]

That gives you a clean path from design intuition, to benchmark discipline, to practical workflow, to specific matching and weighting implementations.
